author: @robertgaines-tulsa
text: Something like ten years ago, I became interested in flower pot heaters. I watched many videos and read different websites before attempting to build my own. Because people were having issues with their pot heaters bursting into flames, I chose to play it safe and use only one candle per heater. You can always make more single candle heaters and spread them around a room if you need to. I was aware that liquid wax could be heated to ignition, so I added extra safety rules like the candle must rest completely below the flower pot with a gap. The heat from the flame will rise into the flower pot so it doesn't need to be enveloped inside the pot. People are really silly by packing candles completely inside a flower pot and are shocked when the candles run away. That's like putting your candles in an oven and expecting everything to be just fine. What I settled on was a single candle flower pot heater with an aluminum stand. I modified the design by bolting a larger pot onto the smaller one to make the heater more efficient an by placing a terracotta dish under the aluminum stand. The aluminum stand is tall enough to provide a gap between the a votive candle holder and the pot while also lifting the candle up off the surface to keep the surface cool. The only problems I've had with this set up is accidentally knocking the flower pot heater over. The candle immediately went out and made a wax mess. I wouldn't want to jinx myself, but in the past ten years the candle wax hasn't self ignited. The heater doesn't produce a lot of heat especially with only one candle, but even with one candle it makes a difference. I use it mainly for supplemental heat as the infrared heat it produces helps to make the room feel more warm. It should also keep me from freezing in a power failure, but I do have a fireplace that I'd likely be using. It's more of novelty heating device, and should be approached as such.

author: @robertgaines-tulsa
text: I had a friend who's (now x) husband thought he could burn his Christmas tree by just slowing shoving it into their fireplace. She told him the whole damn tree is just going to go up in flames, but her husband wouldn't believe her. Rather than argue with him, she went to the kitchen and started filling pots with water. Sure enough, those pots of water were used in short order to put out the fire. Some people just need to understand that pants don't make you hyper intelligent.

author: @slicko_di_poo
text:
As a kid in the army I was once part of a barbeque with a bonfire at a vehicle repair workshop, not that far from a fuel station.

The bonfire wouldn't take for some reason, so someone siphoned petrol from one of the Land Rovers in for work that was near the pyre. He just siphoned directly onto cotton waste, and some of the other guys carried bundles of fuel soaked waste to the wood pile. (It was Huge.) That meant that some petrol leaked onto the ground and found its way to close enough to catch fire later.

Luckily the guy who lit the thing threw something burning in, instead of sticking his arm in and using his cigarette lighter, because it almost exploded. Not quite. It went whoosh, and shot a little volcano of flames up into the sky, higher than the workshop roof. (Workshop was big enough to fit a stack of three buses, one on top of the other under its roof - just as a rough measure of the size of the fire.

Whoosh!

And then the flames started running over the ground toward the vehicles, and down toward the diesel pumps. It was a close call.

Great bonfire, though. Burnt for hours, and we had to stay far away from it.

author: @a.k.4550
text: Excellent information as always! You've brought to mind my own formative memories on test apparatus safety; At about seven years of age, many miles from a library and before the internet even had images, I set out to answer my own question, "will gasoline boil before it catches fire?" The answer was yes, of course. but I also learned that fuel can burn off of a number of surfaces without damaging it, provided the flash/melting point has not been reached. In addition, I learned that I could jump clear over a lawn tractor while carrying a plastic gas can with my shoes on fire. I got the fire put out without causing any damage, sustaining injury or getting caught doing unsanctioned science in the shed.

author: @druidjuicer636
text: I made a two can heater. What a lot of people don't understand is that even in a simple system, there are a lot of variables that really matter. So, I had a problem with my first design in that there was too large a gap between the inner tin and the outer one and the flame propagated to the fuel reservoir. This popped the inner can out and left the reservoir burning. No problem though. It was on top of my wood burner and the wet towel was laid over, shutting everything down in moments. I worked out what was going on and addressed it. So, even a robust design can break down, be out of spec or just do something you don't expect. You've got to have a backup and then a backup to the backup- think "what are my priorities and what am I going to do if...?". Then you learn and learn safely. It's really that simple.

I’ve looked at that guy again and there is so much wrong. I think he needs to stick to counting the pencils at his accountant job the guy is totally incompetent and threw his teddy out because he clearly didn’t understand what he had made. He had made a frigging bomb and caught a load of unburnt gas in the plant pot and then cack handedly failed to remove the top container and nearly knocked over the 1/4 full bottom container. 
He’s advocating also using hot glue to insulate mains connections to those resistive panels he is considering using to heat ceramic tiles. Hot glue and electrical connections where heat is involved. Also dubious about using those panels for the purpose he is advocating … god help him he needs to stick to something safe.

It looks like he failed to make any holes in the inner container and when it goes off like a bomb he repeats the same thing again … he doesn’t even know that he is using methylated spirits .. I honestly feel he’s trying to replicate your channel Bob an when he screws up tries to blame someone else for his incompetence.

I think it is good that you showed how not to do it - but to my mind there was a bit of learning you needed to go through - perhaps you could have highlighted that and made your message a bit more positive - you might even had more views - you know - improvements I have made lesson I have learnt a beginners guide to - I don't know just something positive because what happens when you do 'danger of' is folks will watch to see if you lose your eyebrows - but they don't listen to you - or at least those who need to listen don't - you just collect a group of head nodders with folks who already agree - but nobody learns and isn't that the point? Anyway I am sorry if you are upset.

author: @martineleven8179
text: Hello Robert,  I've finally got the vegetable oil to burn on graphite felt, although not in a pipe or fitting,  I got an old fray bentos pie tin, put vegetable oil in it, then cut a 50mm square piece of 12mm thick felt, pushed a small nail through the bottom of it to stop it falling over, quite happily sits on edge in the centre of the pool, to light hold it on edge,  after about 20-30 secs of holding it on a flame it lit, then place in the middle,  it burnt for about 6 hrs before needing a top up of very little oil,  needs a chimney now  so I'm going to cut the bottom off a demijohn and see how it goes.

author: @aCalmHinduCow
text: I was thinking while you were doing all these great videos with different fuels, flower pot heaters with mantles, etc, that you were obviously overestimating the common sense and safety-mindedness of people nowadays and that there would likely be some accidents or close calls from idiots doing stupid things. It sounds like that premonition played out exactly as expected. I hope we don't see any Darwin Award recipients from all this. Make sure you have a strong disclaimer and warning at the start of each video that might prove dangerous or deadly to a stupid person attempting to replicate your results, and yet who doesn't know WTF they're doing. Keep up the great work. Love your videos.

author: @kennymann3705
text: We could discuss the concept of living in denial. The facing of realities, being predicate to functional awareness and control. And yet not everyone will want to sort how cognitive bias influences living in denial. Uh oh. (There are so many forms of cognitive bias. Who can keep track? Cognitive bias, itself, exists naturally because it's humanly impossible to carry a concept of the whole universe around in our heads. Triage, then.) All of which means we're stuck with defining "common sense" and working out among ourselves how best to apply whatever we define "common sense" to be. Call it a reconciliation. This is why I get carried away on my concern about people living in denial. Because there will always be someone or other whose cognitive bias has them living in denial about whatever we reconcile on as a definition of "common sense" and on its application. The few of us, that is, who are ever willing to reconcile on anything. Not compromise -- everyone facing objective realities that favor no one. <sigh> Fire should be an easy one. Kinda proves the need for concern. Oh well.

replies.author: @kennymann3705
replies.text:
Me: "Hey Aloysius, fire can be dangerous. Did you know that oxidation and its toxic byproducts can rapidly and nearly instantaneously get out of control, given all the readily available possibilities? You know how all that works, right?"
Aloysius: "Fire? So they say. But I don't buy it. What's that got to do with anything? You going camping? If I ever use my fireplace again, I'll let you know. Can't say what my plans are though. I'm gonna be busy with a big pyrotechnic display we're putting on at the county fair all this week. Sparklers in the sky like you've never seen. Not much free time for your elaborate theories."

replies.author: @ClownWhisper
replies.text: @AndreaDingbatt if there was any way that I could move there and keep my disability I would be so thrilled but I don't think there was it would be any way I could afford it I don't receive enough money from my disability insurance. I live in a very modest area as a result of how one of our political parties has trimmed it down what our disabled people receive. I paid a lot into the system I worked in the life sciences pharmaceutical robotics mainly a lot of manual riding towards the end and middle management type of work traveling in Europe traveling in the United states and then finally I was auctioned off to another large life science company great before I got sick then had to stop working so I lived mainly in hotel rooms only on the East Coast consulting for the company that acquired are product line. If there was some way that I could live in the UK for my final years I would be so happy I believe I could anyway. I just can't breathe here I can't relax I feel like I'm out of place I would love to meet Robert one time and shake his hand I'd love to go to Cornwall and walk in my grandfather's footsteps his writings and books and photography are in a small museum I'd like to go see that I tried to acquire photocopies of some of this stuff and they wanted$50 a page plus shipping I was a little put off being the grandson of the author but that's water under the bridge I guess.

author: @Paxmax
text: One thing about starting out small: if you have a new "DIY" burner, do put in enough fuel to saturate the residual air volume with fuel vapors. If you have very little fuel and a big enough air pocket you pretty much built a small *boom!* device. Also, f you re-light a cold burner with an unknown fuel quantity in it, put in some more just to make sure it's vapor saturated.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: And this good advice doesn't just apply to working with fire and combustibles... it applies to all aspects of tinkering and inventing for every subject (Electricity, Chemistry, Physics, Mechanical, Hydraulics, Pneumatics, Biology, anything that can result in harm). Before actually doing "anything"... go study the risk, and dangers, and the proper safety precautions. OSHA guidelines are particular helpful. There's loads of safety videos and books. There's countless dangers out there.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they do - you are supposed to install a hood over a cooker and turn it on when you are cooking - folks just don't

replies.author: @anthonydunn9532
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkeringI have just found out that only since April 2006 has it been a legal requirement to fit an extractor over a cooker in a new build kitchen. My father's gas cooker is in corner of his kitchen and has been for over 60 without an extractor. Maybe that's why he is slowing down at the age of 93!
I find it strange that it has always been a legal requirement for a gas 🔥 but not a cooker. 

Many thanks
Tony.

author: @docink6175
text: Working with flammable materials is dangerous.. it kind of goes without saying but by the same token we need to say something about it when we are doing videos, because not everyone realizes how dangerous it can be. I've been working with flammable liquids for many years and was a volunteer fireman but that doesn't mean dangerous accidents don't happen. Last night I was videoing the different burn times of alcohol-based fuels. I was working in a bed of sand on a wooden table. I accidentally bumped the table hard enough to send fuel flying, luckily it splashed away from me and onto the table or it could have been really bad.. and of course I had more sand and a fire extinguisher on hand.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah it's about experience and control - but can you image if every video had 5 minutes of warning before it started - folks would watch them and that's the problem - this video will get no where near the views the main video got and the main video only got 50% watched - that means folks just skipped through to the 'interesting bit' I don't think we can cover every eventuality - there has to be some personal responsibility

replies.author: @docink6175
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering true, but people dont, especially over here where we are a very litigious lot.  Thats why so many things come with warning labels like a soldering gun that warns its not to be inserted in the body... Or HOT on the side of a hot beverage

author: @denis9450
text: If I was you I would say before you start you video's that you say this is not an instructional video it's just as you would do it as a trained engineer but you suggest that if they want one that they get a trained engineer to do it. Also I was a bit surprised when you did yours you Had your container of ethanol on the bench I would have put it outside after topping up the heater if that flashed off it would have filled your workshop full of flames it's the fumes that ignite been a service and installation Engineer all my life just retired at 69 fairly new to the channel but have loved what I have seen just cover your back.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there is not enough in a bottle of ethanol to do that in the lab mate - the lab is a big open space - I wouldn't have done that in a smaller space that's for sure - but I hate that ass covering - really we should take some responsibility for ourselves too

author: @corringhamdepot4434
text: Recently watched a video about the dangers of plant pot heaters. From a guy who made a hanging heater with 4 tea lights under a plant pot, and hung it up in his narrow boat.  Then discovered that using multiple tea lights very close together, generated enough heat for all of the tea light wax to melt and burst into flames at once.

author: @dr.froghopper6711
text: My parents feared my supposed pyromania when I was younger. I was no maniac. I never started any dangerous fires or took any unnecessary chances. I was only seeking to understand fire and how to control it. These days, my family knows me as The Fire Keeper because I do control the flame in a very difficult and inefficient stove. I keep it burning cleanly and without smoking the house out. They have trouble with both.

author: @Dorpz
text:
Reminds me of how I learned to respect fire about ten years ago!

I was a teenager at the time, old enough to know better, and I saw people making DIY alcohol stoves from old treacle tins so I thought "I can do that" despite not having tins or alcohol.
So naturally, I combine some mince pie tin foil casings, poke holes into them and made a pretty good little stove. I set the stove on my bare thigh (because I'm obviously fireproof and very tough) and fill it with lighter fluid.
Set it going, within less time than it takes for a high performance car to change gears, my leg and my carpet is on fire!
I quickly stomped the carpet out with my bare feet and my leg just sorted itself out, thankfully I didn't get any major burns but that carpet had a mince pie shaped reminder that fire is not something to trifle with. 

Even back then as a silly bastard of a teenager, I knew it wasn't anyone's fault but mine! 

My advice to anyone who wants to play with fire is please absolutely do it, but for the love of god do it outside or on a concrete floor with no combustibles nearby.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good advice and great view mate

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Yup, when playing with fire, one needs to think about how could it get out of control. And think about how they might get it back under control. 

When not experienced, always remember Fine powders have high surface area that might react more quickly thrn expected. Always limit the amount of fuel. Always consider things around the experiment and if they are safe around fire, or may it help a fire get out of control. Always consider if a place is sufficiently ventilated and to the best of ones ability, make an effort to know what  chemicals went in to the products and if they may become poisonous  if put in the air.  

For example Don't burn PVC inside ones home. Breathing in fumes with Chlorine in them is very very bad for ones lungs.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate - cheers

replies.author: @petevenuti7355
replies.text:
A lawyer once explained to me that if a prosecutor could convince a jury that you intended to set some rocks on fire , that you were stupid enough to think rocks could burn,  that's arson and you get a minimum of 7 years jail time... Even if you did no damage nor intended to, but all a prosecutor has to do is convince a jury that you're stupid... And you could spend most of the decade in jail... Hence destroying the life of the person convicted.
 I'm from the USA, this is typical..
 And that screws the good citizens with common sense while someone else is making videos...
